narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways during the admissions review process. The automated system we use to evaluate applications predicted an 82% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background data. This high predicted risk led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those with stronger signals of bar exam success to maintain high pass rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 24, which is notably below the typical applicant's score of around 37 and signaled lower readiness for the rigors ahead. Next in importance was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, far under the average of about 5.5, indicating your performance relative to peers in that later year raised concerns about sustaining progress. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 offered some counterbalance—it's slightly below the average of 3.21 but still nudged the prediction slightly toward passing success compared to even weaker GPAs. These top three elements—LSAT, Year 3 deciles, and undergrad GPA—dominated the decision, outweighing other details like your full-time status, which is common among applicants. In contrast to typical students who pass the bar, your scores placed you in the lower range of the distribution, where historical data shows higher failure rates. While your middle-income background aligns with many in the dataset, it didn't sway the outcome much here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort—it's a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your Year 3 performance through targeted study or extracurriculars, and perhaps exploring undergrad GPA improvement options like post-bac courses. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at this or other schools. Keep pushing; many have turned similar profiles around with focused preparation.
